pageTitle.value: Dianne Reeves & Song Yi Jeon - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: For Grammy-winning jazz singer Dianne Reeves, the best way to mentor South Korean jazz composer and singer Song Yi Jeon was to invite her to perform with her band.

WaPageTitle.value: Dianne Reeves & Song Yi Jeon - Rolex mentoring programme

metadescription.value: Grammy-winner Dianne Reeves is helping her protégée, jazz composer Song Yi Jeon, develop a deeper understanding of herself as an artist. More on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Dianne Reeves & Song Yi Jeon - Rolex mentoring programme

text.value: For Grammy-winning jazz singer Dianne Reeves, the best way to mentor South Korean jazz composer and singer Song Yi Jeon was to invite her to perform with her band. Song Yi Jeon joined her on two European tours and in the process developed a deeper understanding of herself as an artist. Here, they each relate how they experienced their relationship.

“I will never forget the first day I met her in New York. Dianne Reeves, Miss Dianne Reeves, Grammy-winning vocalist – had listened to all of my music. And immediately understood what I wanted to say with it. I was amazed by that. Somebody who’s won five Grammys, who is an idol of mine, was telling me, ‘I know this music.’ She just blew my mind.

Dianne has given me so much. She doesn’t tell me what is right or wrong, she waits until I ask a question, or make a suggestion, and she listens, and we talk. She’s very thoughtful. She has helped me have deeper love for what I do. She gave me this experience of standing in front of tons of people at festivals and concerts during tours in Europe. She gave me the confidence to believe that I can do anything, that I can be anything, and that what I do is good.

She introduced me so many people, so many places and things. And she had so many stories about how she started her career – the managers, the musicians. In my family, nobody’s a musician – I didn’t start jazz until I was in my 20s.

I had the idea of using traditional Korean percussion music as a base for jazz music, so I started listening to it, and created my own groove lines, then wrote music on top of them. I showed it to Dianne and even though I only said one or two things about how I created it, she understood why I made it, beyond just, ‘Oh because I like it’, but because of much deeper cultural reasons.

For a long time, I was using music as a tool to achieve something, which meant I wasn’t really appreciating what music is, what art is. Slowly over the last couple of years, I’ve been getting rid of this eagerness to be acknowledged as an amazing singer, and more as an artist. Sometimes I collaborate with a dancer or a classical musician and try free improvisation, which means trying to understand myself from the inside and not from the perspective of other people.

I want to keep searching for something that I don’t know yet, and I would like to teach students so I can pass on everything I’m getting from Dianne. I want to keep myself creative. It’s a cycle, and I’d like to keep that cycle going for the rest of my life. That’s my dream.”

title.value: I want to keep searching for something that I don’t know yet, and I would like to teach students so I can pass on everything I’m getting from Dianne.

text.value:
“I grew up in a multi-generational household. We had musicians in my family; either people performed professionally, or they just played great records. Charles Burrell was my first mentor. He helped me to listen to music in a deeper way – not just the words, but the choices and the uniqueness of each voice.

I spent time listening to Song Yi’s music and really taking it in. I decided to get her on stage. She’s an impeccable musician. We would start her off in different scenarios, doing electronic things or working with my guitarist, or with me and the band, and she had no problem taking on every one of those things. That was exciting. She did a couple of tours with me and felt so much a part of us.

The beauty of music is that it is a language and improvisation allows you to connect on another spiritual level. With Song Yi, it has no boundaries, it’s limitless. We’ll just start something and I know she will pick up. We are both in this music and it’s like, okay, let’s talk.

Her music has no words, but I understand it. She’s brought it back to the first and most powerful instrument, which is the voice, and I listen to it like I would any other instrumentalist. The way she can shape her voice, the colours, the timbre, and just the sound, to get her point across without words, is really something. She’s a brilliant composer and has a wonderful eye for photography, and images, and how all they play together.

title.value: Her music has no words, but I understand it. She’s brought it back to the first and most powerful instrument, which is the voice, and I listen to it like I would any other instrumentalist.

text.value:
She’s very prolific in her writing, constantly creating, but you need a platform, a place that’s peaceful, and open and welcoming. That was the space that I was able to make for her. I gave her an opportunity because she was bursting at the seams – ready to open up and make music. There’s such a freshness about the way that she approaches it, and more than anything, it is her voice, her unique voice.

It took me a long time to find myself, but I see a lot of young people come in and that is where they start. There’s no industry telling you what to do. Now you’re allowed to be in your fullness.”

pageTitle.value: Anne Lacaton & Arine Aprahamian - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: Young Lebanese-Armenian architect Arine Aprahamian is inspired to create buildings that are innovative, affordable and sustainable. Her philosophy sits well with French Pritzker-Prize winning architect Anne Lacaton, who espouses building renewal over demolition.
